coredata.dc:title: Study of electrochromic behavior of the supporting electrolyte free electrochromic devices based on the graphene quantum dots functionalized viologens for energy-saving smart window application
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Jamdegni, Monika
Kaur, Amarjeet
coredata.dc:description:
Here in this work, two electrochromic systems have been prepared by functionalizing di-pentyl viologen (DPV) and di-heptyl viologen (DHV) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Strong molecular forces like cation-
π
interactions, π–π stacking interactions, electrostatic interactions, etc., ensure an adequate interaction between GQDs and viologens, hence, responsible for stabilizing the electrochromic system. All solid electrolyte-free electrochromic devices have been fabricated by dispersing DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD in poly(vinyl)alcohol (PVA) gel. Electrochemical behavior and electrochromic responses of the fabricated devices were investigated using chronoamperometry, cyclic voltammetry and in situ UV–vis spectroscopy. Both devices were found to work efficiently without the help of supporting electrolytes. Both DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD devices exhibit high optical contrast of 53.4% and 60%, with superior coloration efficiency of 143.9 cm2/C and 66 cm2/C, respectively. Swift electrochromic responses of 4.4 s/3.5 s and 6.2 s/2.5 s have been recorded for coloration/bleaching for DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD devices, respectively. Fabricated DPV-GQD device was found to retain 73% of its initial optical contrast while DHV-GQD depicts retention of only 63% after 3000 cycles. The superior coloration efficiency and balanced response time of DPV-GQD make it a potential material for the device application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Viologen
Gel electrolyte
Electrochromic device
Chromophore
Smart windows

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of a supporting electrolyte free GQD functionalized viologen electrochromic system.
The schematic diagram for the electrochromic gel-based device.
Infrared spectra of a) 1,1′-dipentyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (DPV) and b) 1,1′-diheptyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (DHV).
NMR Spectroscopy of a) 1,1′-dipentyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (DPV) and b) 1,1′-diheptyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (DHV).
Cyclic Voltammetry of a) DPV-GQD and b) DHV-GQD.
Three electrode configuration with Ag/AgCl reference electrode.
Chronoamperometry and transmittance curves for the fabricated DPV-GQD (a) coloration, (b) bleaching and DHV-GQD (c) coloration, (d) bleaching devices.
Optical stability of DPV-GQD (a,b) and DHV-GQD (c,d) devices.
Electrochemical Stability of DPV-GQD (a,b) and DHV-GQD (c,d) devices.
Images of colored and bleached state of DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD device on (a) ITO glass, (b) ITO PET.
Bending image of the fabricated ITO PET device.

head.ce:title: Study of electrochromic behavior of the supporting electrolyte free electrochromic devices based on the graphene quantum dots functionalized viologens for energy-saving smart window application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Here in this work, two electrochromic systems have been prepared by functionalizing di-pentyl viologen (DPV) and di-heptyl viologen (DHV) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Strong molecular forces like cation-
π
interactions, π–π stacking interactions, electrostatic interactions, etc., ensure an adequate interaction between GQDs and viologens, hence, responsible for stabilizing the electrochromic system. All solid electrolyte-free electrochromic devices have been fabricated by dispersing DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD in poly(vinyl)alcohol (PVA) gel. Electrochemical behavior and electrochromic responses of the fabricated devices were investigated using chronoamperometry, cyclic voltammetry and in situ UV–vis spectroscopy. Both devices were found to work efficiently without the help of supporting electrolytes. Both DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD devices exhibit high optical contrast of 53.4% and 60%, with superior coloration efficiency of 143.9 cm2/C and 66 cm2/C, respectively. Swift electrochromic responses of 4.4 s/3.5 s and 6.2 s/2.5 s have been recorded for coloration/bleaching for DPV-GQD and DHV-GQD devices, respectively. Fabricated DPV-GQD device was found to retain 73% of its initial optical contrast while DHV-GQD depicts retention of only 63% after 3000 cycles. The superior coloration efficiency and balanced response time of DPV-GQD make it a potential material for the device application.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic (EC) materials are recognized for their competence in exhibiting reversible optical change through their redox reactions when the device electrodes are subjected to a suitable bias. In addition to the modulation of optical properties, electrochromic devices (ECDs) exhibit characteristic low voltage operation and low power consumption [1–9]. Comprising fascinating optical properties, ECDs have drawn the attention of commercial companies and researchers worldwide[10]. In the past few years, electrochromic materials have been employed for diverse applications such as multicolor EC displays for less power consumption, anti-glare car rear-view mirrors, energy-saving smart windows, protective eyewear, electrochromic supercapacitors [11,12]. Generally, in addition to the electrochromic layer having conducting polymers or metal oxides, the EC device structure is a combination of multiple layers such as ion-conductive electrolyte and ion storage layer. In contrast, a much simpler device configuration consisting of an EC layer between two electrodes can be achieved from small organic molecules because redox-active electrochromic species can be dispersed in the electrolyte layer [4,8,13].

Out of several electrochromic material families, well-known cathodic coloring viologen derivatives (1,1′-disubstituted-4,4′- bipyridinium salt) provide high transmittance changes. Most of them exhibit three redox states, namely, di-cation, radical cation, and di-reduced units [14–19].
V
2 +
+
e
−
→
V
1 + •
V
1 + •
+
e
−
→
V
o

More importantly, the presence of nitrogen atoms offers the incorporation of various substituents in viologen for a controllable color change of ECDs [7,8,10,12]. In connection with chromophore (electrochrome) solubility, ECDs can be distinguished into three types. A type-I electrochrome, during the electrochromic process, remains in the solution at all times while type-II electrochromic materials do not exhibit color in the solution but after an electron transfer, a solid color is formed on the surface of the electrode. Unlike types(I and II), type-III electrochromic materials always remain on the surface of the electrode usually in a thin film form [8,20].

Furthermore, electrolytes can be classified into liquid electrolytes, gel electrolytes and solid electrolytes based on their state. The leakage problem of the liquid electrolytes results in the poor long-term stability of ECDs while the ion mobility of solid electrolytes is lower, gel electrolytes used in ECDs are a good alternative. An ECD involving a single electroactive layer (“all-in-one”) of gel electrolyte(GE) has plenty of advantages [21]. The GE-based device can avoid the issues(such as risks of leaking, presence of bubbles and some safety concerns) inherent in liquid electrolytes [22]. Major concerns like slow response and less transparency can be eliminated by utilizing gel. Along with the simplification of flexible device manufacturing, gel electrolytes lead to large-area fabrication [2,5,21].

Herein, we investigate synthesized 1,1′-dipentyl-4,4′-bipyridinium (DPV) dibromide and 1,1′-diheptyl-4,4′-bipyridinium (DHV)dibromide electrochromic materials functionalized with the graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) (abbreviated as DPV-GQDs and DHV-GQDs, respectively). Strong intermolecular forces between the viologen and GQDs aid to become a complement to each other. Cation – π electron interactions, π–π stacking interactions and electrostatic interactions are accountable for stabilizing the viologen-GQD system [8,23]. Conjugation of viologen with graphene helps to discard the use of supporting electrolytes e.g. KBr, KCl, etc., which are widely used with the viologens. Fig. 1 illustrates the supporting electrolyte-free electrochromic system containing viologen and GQDs only. As the viologen exists in a cationic (V2+) state, hence its electron deficiency is overcome by the electron-donating properties of the GQDs.

The oxygen functional groups in the GQDs can electrostatically bind with viologen molecules. The benzene rings of the viologen can attach to the GQD rings by face-to-face stacking. 1,1′-dipentyl-4,4′-bipyridinium (DPV) and 1,1′-diheptyl-4,4′-bipyridinium(DHV) functionalization with GQDs provide a future direction towards the much-simplified composition for flexible electrochromic devices [23].

4,4′−Dipyridine (C10H8N2) 98%, 1-Bromopentane (CH3(CH2)4Br) 98%, 1-Bromoheptane (C7H15Br) 99%, Acetonitrile(ACN), graphene quantum dots(1 mg/ml), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (146,000–186,000) 99% hydrolyzed were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as such without further purification. For the transparent electrodes, ITO-coated glass with 10–15 Ω cm−2 was used. Before device fabrication, the ITO glass substrate was cleaned with the help of detergent and de-ionized (DI) water, followed by sonication in methanol followed by acetone.

1,1′-dipentyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (yield approx. 81.7%) and 1,1′-diheptyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dibromide (yield approx. 84.2%) were synthesized from 4,4′-bipyridyl. 1 mol of 4,4′-bipyridyl dissolved in acetonitrile followed by 3 moles of excessive addition (greater than 2 moles) of 1- bromoheptane for DHV (and 1-bromopentane for DPV). The prepared solution was continuously stirred and refluxed at a temperature 80 °C for 48 h [7,24,25]. As the reaction proceeds, a yellow precipitate started to form. After completion of the reaction, this yellow product was filtered and washed several times in ACN. Finally, the material was dried for two days in a vacuum at room temperature. This shining yellow powder was used for further investigations.

To synthesize the EC gel, the chromophore (DPV/DHV) along with GQD was dispersed in the PVA gel matrix. For this purpose, 0.4 g of PVA (final amount 10% weight) is added to 4 ml of DI water containing 50 mM DPV/DHV and 0.4 ml of GQD (final amount 0.1 mg/ml in solution) and continuously stirred for 12 h. Two ITO-coated glasses were used to sandwich the EC gel as depicted in Fig. 2 and stuck together with the help of a double-sided highly chemically-inert tape to avoid leakage.

DPV and DHV were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50 FT-IR) in the wavelength range 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. Further, proton (1H) NMR was recorded by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) Brooker Avance (400 MHz) instrument. Electrochemical studies such as cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry were carried out by Potentiostat/Galvanostat Autolab(PGSTAT30N) setup using software Nova 1.11. For optical and electrochromic properties UV-vis spectra (300–700 nm) were recorded using an Autolab assembly Avantes spectrophotometer.

FT-IR spectroscopy has been investigated to determine the C-N bonding arose by attachment of the alkane chain to the 4,4′-bipyridinium unit. 4,4′-bipyridinium alone shows aromatic C-N bonding corresponding to the peak 1278 cm−1 ( Fig. 3a and b). Thus the presence of an extra peak around 1174 cm−1 (Fig. 3a and b) is realized as new C-N bonding in synthesized DPV and DHV. Further C-H bondings (around 3000 cm−1) in DPV and DHV (Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b) spectra ascribed the occurrence of both sp2 and sp3 carbons earlier which were exclusively missing in 4,4′-bipyridinium and bromopentane/bromoheptane. ( Fig. 4).

Furthermore, 1H NMR of both the materials DPV and DHV has been carried out to validate the synthesis. DPV:1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.43 (d, J = 6.4 Hz, 4 H), 8.82 (d, J = 6.3 Hz, 4 H), 4.71 (t, J = 7.5 Hz, 4 H), 1.99 (p, J = 7.5 Hz, 4 H), 1.35 (dt, J = 10.7, 6.3 Hz, 5 H), 1.30 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 3 H), 0.89 (t, J = 6.9 Hz, 6 H). DHV: 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.45 (d, J = 6.1 Hz, 4 H), 8.83 (d, J = 6.1 Hz, 4 H), 4.73 (t, J = 7.5 Hz, 4 H), 1.99 (p, J = 7.1 Hz, 4 H), 1.36 – 1.28 (m, 11 H), 1.28 – 1.22 (m, 5 H), 0.87 (t, J = 6.8 Hz, 6 H).

Fig. 5 portrays the typical electrochemical behavior for DPV-GQD (Fig. 5a) and DHV-GQD (Fig. 5b) materials. To carry out the investigation 50 mM of DPV/DHV is mixed with GQD (0.1 mg/ml final weight percentage) in DI water and stirred for 12 h. The prepared solution is analyzed using a three-electrode setup (
Fig. 6). Initially being in a dicationic state both the materials perform a reduction around −0.6 V, capturing one of the electrons. This causes a change from a dicationic state to a cationic state and the reduction is attributed to coloration in these nanocomposites. GQDs are expected to reduce the solution resistance like an electrolyte and support the reduction-oxidation of the chromophore by charge transfer.

Each electrochromic system was subjected to a persistent voltage of −0.7 V (in a three-electrode setup) and −2.5 V (for device) each for the 30 s, thus exhibiting characteristics illustrated in Fig. 7. The UV transmission spectra were recorded for fully colored and bleached states for both devices. It was perceived that when the electrochromic device changes from transparent to a colored state then it blocks a certain wavelength region by changing its dicationic state (V2+) to a radical cationic (V1+•) state.

Response time is the time taken by the device to change its redox states (coloration and bleaching process) on applying the potential. In the present investigations, the response time has been calculated from the chronoamperometric i-t curve (Fig. 7) as the time corresponds to the 90% current value of its full switch. Response time for the DPV-GQD device (4.4 s/3.5 s) was found to be better for coloration and bleached states, than that of the DHV-GQD device (6.2 s/2.5 s). This may be due to the somewhat faster diffusion of ions in the DPV-GQD device than in the DHV-GQD device. Furthermore, response time can be calculated from the optical changes where response time refers to the time required to bring a change of 90% in its transmittance [26]. In the case of transmittance, the DPV-GQD device exhibits a response time of 8.82 s/8.1 s while the DHV-GQD device has a response of 11.8 s/6.4 s. The higher value of response time is achieved from transmittance data than that of chronoamperometry because current changes are rapid in the chronoamperometric curves in comparison to the transmittance.

Optical contrast, one of the key features of electrochromic devices, is generally measured as maximum transmittance change during the redox process at a particular wavelength (λ). It is defined as the absolute percent transmittance difference (ΔT%) between the fully oxidized and fully reduced states. As observed in Fig 8., for DPV-GQD significant optical contrast of 53.4% is observed at 505 nm while the DHV-GQD device shows a gradual change of transmittance at 534 nm with an optical contrast of 60%.

Evaluation of the power consumption of the fabricated electrochromic device was done using a practical tool i.e. coloration efficiency (CE), defined as the ratio of optical density to the charge injected/ejected. A higher value of coloration efficiency leads to a lower amount of charge consumed during the coloration of the electrochromic device. C.E. was premeditated using the mathematical definition [27–32].
CE ( η ) =
Δ OD
σ
where σ is the charge (Q) to active device area (A) ratio. Charge consumed to produce coloration on an electrode surface can be extracted from the integration of the area under the corresponding chronoamperometric (i-t) curve
Q =
∫
i
dt
and change in optical density (ΔOD) is attributed as
Δ OD = ln
T
b
/
T
c
where Tb and Tc respectively correspond to the bleached and colored state of the examined device at a wavelength(λ). Coloration efficiency for the DPV-GQD device is found to be 143.9 cm2/C which is far higher than the DHV-GQD system (66 cm2/C).

For the electrochromic device application, the retentivity of optical contrast is one of the important factors. To investigate this, both of the fabricated devices alternatively switched in the colored and bleached state for 3000 cycles keeping each state for the 30 s. It was found that after 3000 cycles DHV-GQD system retains 62% of its initial optical contrast (60%). On the other hand, the DPV-GQD device exhibiting 39% optical contrast after 3000 cycles maintains 73% of its initial value. The stabilization may be due to the synergistic effect of the GQD honeycomb-like structure which facilitates the reduction process due to its electron-donating nature. On the other hand, during the oxidation process, immediate electron transfer takes place from viologen to GQDs and hence stabilizes the composite, delocalizing the charge over the several benzene rings of GQDs.

To investigate the electrochemical stability, fabricated devices were continuously switched in the alternative colored and bleached state as demonstrated in Fig. 9. Each state was kept for the 30 s and repeated for 3000 cycles. From peak to peak current analysis, it was found that the DPV-GQD device retains 41% of the initial current density while the current density of DHV-GQD is reduced by 73% after 3000 cycles. The reduction in current density may be attributed to the degradation of the polymer gel matrix into oligomers and the formation of dimerized viologen cations. Di-reduced cation will combine with the dication of viologen and forms the radical viologen cation which resists the diffusion of electrochromes leading to a decrease in electrochemical stability (Fig. 9). Fabricated devices in the two-electrode configuration have been presented in Fig. 10. For the flexible device application, color and the bleached state of the device have been realized using ITO coated PET electrode. Fig. 11 depicts the bending of the device.

Viologen-GQD composite-based devices have been fabricated by synthesizing Di-pentyl viologen bromide and Di-heptyl viologen bromide and various electrochromic features of these all-in-one gel-based EC devices have been investigated. Such kind of nanocomposites have ease of electron transfer for reduction of viologen utilizing electron-donating properties of GQDs. Due to various electrostatic interactions between viologen and GQDs, these devices exhibit a very stable electrochromic performance. Fabricated devices are found to work efficiently in their solid-state form which overcomes the liquid electrolyte problem. Both the device maintains their quite good optical contrast 73% (for DPV-GQD device) and 63% (for DHV-GQD device) for 3000 cycles. Furthermore, these materials have been employed on ITO-coated PET which supports the application of such electrochromic systems for flexible EC devices. Recent advances in the field of electrochromic devices depict the device structure simplification by mixing chromophore and supporting electrolytes in a gel medium. The uses of these graphene-based materials direct us to further simplify the device structure. Reduction in one of the components, i.e. supporting electrolyte, illustrates the future direction of supporting electrolyte-free electrochromic devices.

Amit Kumar: Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Amarjeet Kaur: Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Monika Jamdegni: Methodology and Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India for financial support through the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) project EMR/2016/002316. One of the authors (Amit Kumar) is grateful to University Grant Commission (UGC) for the NET-JRF fellowship.
